}

}

}

@media print {
  *,
  *:before,
  *:after {
    color: #000 !important;
    text-shadow: none !important;
    background: transparent !important;
    -webkit-box-shadow: none !important;
            box-shadow: none !important;
  }
  a,
  a:visited {
    text-decoration: underline;
  }
  a[href]:after {
    content: " (" attr(href) ")";
  }
  abbr[title]:after {
    content: " (" attr(title) ")";
  }
  a[href^="#"]:after,
  a[href^="javascript:"]:after {
    content: "";
  }
  pre,
  blockquote {
    border: 1px solid #999;

    page-break-inside: avoid;
  }
  thead {
    display: table-header-group;
  }
  tr,
  img {
    page-break-inside: avoid;
  }
  img {
    max-width: 100% !important;
  }
  p,
  h2,
  h3 {
    orphans: 3;
    widows: 3;
  }
  h2,
  h3 {
    page-break-after: avoid;
  }
  .navbar {
    display: none;
  }
  .btn > .caret,
  .dropup > .btn > .caret {
    border-top-color: #000 !important;
  }
  .label {
    border: 1px solid #000;
  }
  .table {
    border-collapse: collapse !important;
  }
  .table td,
  .table th {
    background-color: #fff !important;
  }
  .table-bordered th,
  .table-bordered td {
    border: 1px solid #ddd !important;
  }
}

.breadcrumb {
  padding: 8px 15px;
  margin-bottom: 20px;
  list-style: none;
  background-color: #f5f5f5;
  border-radius: 4px;
}
.breadcrumb > li {
  display: inline-block;
}
.breadcrumb > li + li:before {
  padding: 0 5px;
  color: #ccc;
  content: "/\A0";
}
.breadcrumb > .active {
  color: #777;
}
.pagination {
  display: inline-block;
  padding-left: 0;
  margin: 20px 0;
  border-radius: 4px;
}
.pagination > li {
  display: inline;
}
.pagination > li > a,
.pagination > li > span {
  position: relative;
  float: left;
  padding: 6px 12px;
  margin-left: -1px;
  line-height: 1.42857143;
  color: #337ab7;
  text-decoration: none;
  background-color: #fff;
  border: 1px solid #ddd;
}
.pagination > li:first-child > a,
.pagination > li:first-child > span {
  margin-left: 0;
  border-top-left-radius: 4px;
  border-bottom-left-radius: 4px;
}
.pagination > li:last-child > a,
.pagination > li:last-child > span {
  border-top-right-radius: 4px;
  border-bottom-right-radius: 4px;
}
.pagination > li > a:hover,
.pagination > li > span:hover,
.pagination > li > a:focus,
.pagination > li > span:focus {
  z-index: 2;
  color: #23527c;
  background-color: #eee;
  border-color: #ddd;
}
.pagination > .active > a,
.pagination > .active > span,
.pagination > .active > a:hover,
.pagination > .active > span:hover,
.pagination > .active > a:focus,
.pagination > .active > span:focus {
  z-index: 3;
  color: #fff;
  cursor: default;
  background-color: #337ab7;
  border-color: #337ab7;
}
.pagination > .disabled > span,
.pagination > .disabled > span:hover,
.pagination > .disabled > span:focus,
.pagination > .disabled > a,
.pagination > .disabled > a:hover,
.pagination > .disabled > a:focus {
  color: #777;
  cursor: not-allowed;
  background-color: #fff;
  border-color: #ddd;
}

  padding: 10px 16px;

  line-height: 1.3333333;
}
.pagination-lg > li:first-child > a,

  border-bottom-left-radius: 6px;

  border-top-left-radius: 3px;

.pagination-sm > li:last-child > a,

  border-top-right-radius: 3px;

@-webkit-keyframes progress-bar-stripes {
  from {
    background-position: 40px 0;
  }
  to {
    background-position: 0 0;
  }
}
@-o-keyframes progress-bar-stripes {
  from {
    background-position: 40px 0;
  }
  to {
    background-position: 0 0;
  }
}
@keyframes progress-bar-stripes {
  from {
    background-position: 40px 0;
  }
  to {
    background-position: 0 0;
  }
}
.progress {
  height: 20px;
  margin-bottom: 20px;
  overflow: hidden;
  background-color: #f5f5f5;
  border-radius: 4px;
  -webkit-box-shadow: inset 0 1px 2px rgba(0, 0, 0, .1);
          box-shadow: inset 0 1px 2px rgba(0, 0, 0, .1);
}
.progress-bar {
  float: left;
  width: 0;
  height: 100%;
  font-size: 12px;
  line-height: 20px;
  color: #fff;
  text-align: center;
  background-color: #337ab7;
  -webkit-box-shadow: inset 0 -1px 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, .15);
          box-shadow: inset 0 -1px 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, .15);
  -webkit-transition: width .6s ease;
       -o-transition: width .6s ease;
          transition: width .6s ease;
}
.progress-striped .progress-bar,
.progress-bar-striped {
  background-image: -webkit-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:      -o-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:         linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  -webkit-background-size: 40px 40px;
          background-size: 40px 40px;
}
.progress.active .progress-bar,
.progress-bar.active {
  -webkit-animation: progress-bar-stripes 2s linear infinite;
       -o-animation: progress-bar-stripes 2s linear infinite;
          animation: progress-bar-stripes 2s linear infinite;
}
.progress-bar-success {
  background-color: #5cb85c;
}
.progress-striped .progress-bar-success {
  background-image: -webkit-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:      -o-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:         linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
}
.progress-bar-info {

}
.progress-striped .progress-bar-info {
  background-image: -webkit-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:      -o-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:         linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
}
.progress-bar-warning {

}

  background-image:      -o-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:         linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
}

.embed-responsive {
  position: relative;
  display: block;
  height: 0;
  padding: 0;
  overflow: hidden;
}
.embed-responsive .embed-responsive-item,
.embed-responsive iframe,
.embed-responsive embed,
.embed-responsive object,
.embed-responsive video {
  position: absolute;
  top: 0;
  bottom: 0;
  left: 0;
  width: 100%;
  height: 100%;
  border: 0;
}
.embed-responsive-16by9 {
  padding-bottom: 56.25%;
}
.embed-responsive-4by3 {
  padding-bottom: 75%;
}
.well {
  min-height: 20px;
  padding: 19px;
  margin-bottom: 20px;
  background-color: #f5f5f5;
  border: 1px solid #e3e3e3;
  border-radius: 4px;
  -webkit-box-shadow: inset 0 1px 1px rgba(0, 0, 0, .05);
          box-shadow: inset 0 1px 1px rgba(0, 0, 0, .05);
}

  border-color: #ddd;
  border-color: rgba(0, 0, 0, .15);
}
.well-lg {
  padding: 24px;
  border-radius: 6px;
}
.well-sm {
  padding: 9px;
  border-radius: 3px;
}
.close {
  float: right;
  font-size: 21px;
  font-weight: bold;
  line-height: 1;
  color: #000;
  text-shadow: 0 1px 0 #fff;
  filter: alpha(opacity=20);
  opacity: .2;
}
.close:hover,
.close:focus {
  color: #000;
  text-decoration: none;
  cursor: pointer;
  filter: alpha(opacity=50);
  opacity: .5;
}

Bug Reports</a></li><li role="presentation" class=""><a href="http://github.com/jzillmann/pdf-to-markdown" target="_blank" role="menuitem" tabindex="-1"> Code @Github</a></li><li role="separator" class="divider"></li><li role="presentation" class=""><a aria-describedby="popover-trigger-click-root-close" role="menuitem" tabindex="-1" href="https://pdf2md.morethan.io/#"> About</a></li></ul></div><ul class="nav nav-tabs navbar-nav navbar-right"><li role="presentation" class=""><a role="button" href="https://pdf2md.morethan.io/#">Debug View</a></li><li role="presentation" class="active"><a role="button" href="https://pdf2md.morethan.io/#">Result View</a></li></ul></div><div class="navbar-collapse collapse"><p class="navbar-text navbar-right">Deep reinforcement learning meets graph neural networks: Exploring a routing optimization use case</p></div></div></nav><div class="container"><div><div><div role="toolbar" class="btn-toolbar"><div class="btn-group btn-group-md"><button type="button" class="btn btn-default">Edit</button><button type="button" class="active btn btn-default">Preview</button></div></div><hr><div><pre><code>Computer Communications 196 (2022) 184–

</code></pre>
<h1>Computer Communications</h1>
<pre><code>journal homepage: http://www.elsevier.com/locate/comcom
</code></pre>
<h2>Deep reinforcement learning meets graph neural networks: Exploring a</h2>
<h2>routing optimization use case</h2>
<h2>Paul Almasana,∗, José Suárez-Varelaa, Krzysztof Rusekb,a, Pere Barlet-Rosa,</h2>
<h2>Albert Cabellos-Aparicioa</h2>
<p>a <em>Barcelona Neural Networking Center, Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya, Barcelona, Spain</em><br>
b <em>Institute of Telecommunications, AGH University of Science and Technology, Krakow, Poland</em></p>
<h2>A R T I C L E I N F O</h2>
<p><em>Keywords:</em><br>
Graph neural networks<br>
Deep reinforcement learning<br>
Routing<br>
Optimization</p>
<h2>A B S T R A C T</h2>
<pre><code>Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has shown a dramatic improvement in decision-making and automated
control problems. Consequently, DRL represents a promising technique to efficiently solve many relevant
optimization problems (e.g., routing) in self-driving networks. However, existing DRL-based solutions applied
to networking fail to generalize, which means that they are not able to operate properly when applied to
network topologies not observed during training. This lack of generalization capability significantly hinders the
deployment of DRL technologies in production networks. This is because state-of-the-art DRL-based networking
solutions use standard neural networks (e.g., fully connected, convolutional), which are not suited to learn from
information structured as graphs.
In this paper, we integrate Graph Neural Networks (GNN) into DRL agents and we design a problem specific
action space to enable generalization. GNNs are Deep Learning models inherently designed to generalize over
graphs of different sizes and structures. This allows the proposed GNN-based DRL agent to learn and generalize
over arbitrary network topologies. We test our DRL+GNN agent in a routing optimization use case in optical
networks and evaluate it on 180 and 232 unseen synthetic and real-world network topologies respectively.
The results show that the DRL+GNN agent is able to outperform state-of-the-art solutions in topologies never
seen during training.
</code></pre>
<p><strong>1. Introduction</strong></p>
<p>In the last years, industrial advances (e.g., Industry 4.0, IoT) and<br>
changes in social behavior created a proliferation of modern network<br>
applications (e.g., Vehicular Networks, AR/VR, Real-Time Communica-<br>
tions), imposing new requirements on backbone networks (e.g., high<br>
throughput and low latency). Consequently, network operators need<br>
to efficiently manage the network resources, ensuring the customer’s<br>
Quality of Service and fulfilling the Service Level Agreements. This is<br>
typically done using expert knowledge or solvers leveraging Integer<br>
Linear Programming (ILP) or Constraint Programming (CP). However,<br>
real-world production networks have in the order of hundreds of nodes<br>
and solvers based on ILP or CP would take a large amount of time to<br>
solve network optimization problems [1,2]. In addition, heuristic based<br>
solutions are far from being optimal.<br>
Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has shown significant im-<br>
provements in sequential decision-making and automated control prob-<br>
lems [3,4]. As a result, the network community is already investigating<br>
DRL as a key technology for network optimization (e.g., routing) with</p>
<p>∗Corresponding author.<br>
<em>E-mail addresses:</em> felician.paul.almasan@upc.edu (P. Almasan), jose.suarez-varela@upc.edu (J. Suárez-Varela), krusek@agh.edu.pl (K. Rusek),<br>
pere.barlet@upc.edu (P. Barlet-Ros), alberto.cabellos@upc.edu (A. Cabellos-Aparicio).</p>
<pre><code>the goal of enabling self-driving networks [5–8]. However, existing
DRL-based solutions still fail to generalize when applied to different
network scenarios [9,10]. In this context, generalization refers to the
ability of the DRL agent to adapt to new network scenarios not seen
during training (e.g., network topologies, configurations).
We argue that generalization is an essential property for the suc-
cessful adoption of DRL technologies in production networks. Without
generalization, DRL solutions should be trained in the same network
where they are deployed, which is not possible or affordable in gen-
eral. To train a DRL agent is a very costly and lengthy process. It
often requires significant computing power and instrumentation of the
network to observe its performance (e.g., delay, jitter). Additionally,
decisions made by a DRL agent during training can lead to degraded
performance or even to service disruption. Thus, training a DRL agent
in the customer’s network may be unfeasible.
With generalization, a DRL agent can be trained with multiple,
representative network topologies and configurations. Afterwards, it
can be applied to other topologies and configurations, as long as they
</code></pre>
<p>https://doi.org/10.1016/j.comcom.2022.09.<br>
Received 3 December 2021; Received in revised form 22 June 2022; Accepted 29 September 2022<br>
Available online 6 October 2022<br>
0140-3664/©2022 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.</p>
<p>share some common properties. Such a ‘‘universal’’ model can be<br>
trained in a laboratory and later on be incorporated in a product<br>
or a network device (e.g., router, load balancer). The resulting solu-<br>
tion would be ready to be deployed to a production network with-<br>
out requiring any further training or instrumentation in the customer<br>
network.^1<br>
Unfortunately, existing DRL proposals for networking were designed<br>
to operate in the same network topology seen during training [9,11,<br>
12], thereby limiting their potential deployment on production net-<br>
works. The main reason behind this strong limitation is that computer<br>
networks are fundamentally represented as graphs. For instance, the<br>
network topology and routing policy are typically represented as such.<br>
However, state-of-the-art proposals [11,13–15] use traditional neural<br>
network (NN) architectures (e.g., fully connected, convolutional) that<br>
are not well suited to model graph-structured information [16].<br>
In this paper, we integrate Graph Neural Networks (GNN) [17]<br>
into DRL agents to solve network optimization problems. Particularly,<br>
our architecture is intended to solve routing optimization in opti-<br>
cal networks and to generalize over never-seen arbitrary topologies.<br>
The GNN integrated in our DRL agent is inspired by Message-passing<br>
Neural Networks (MPNN), which were successfully applied to solve<br>
a relevant chemistry-related problem [18]. In our case, the GNN was<br>
specifically designed to capture meaningful information about the re-<br>
lations between the links and the traffic flowing through the network<br>
topologies.<br>
The evaluation results show that our agent achieves a strong gener-<br>
alization capability compared to state-of-the-art DRL (SoA DRL) algo-<br>
rithms [15]. Additionally, to further test the generalization capability<br>
of the proposed DRL-based architecture, we evaluated it in a set with<br>
232 different real-world network topologies. The results show that<br>
the proposed DRL+GNN architecture is able to achieve outstanding<br>
performance over the networks never seen during training. Finally, we<br>
explore the generalization limitations of our architecture and discuss<br>
its scalability properties.<br>
Overall, our DRL+GNN architecture for network optimization has<br>
the following features:</p>
<ul>
<li><em>Generality:</em> It can work effectively in network topologies and<br>
scenarios never seen during training.</li>
<li><em>Deployability:</em> It can be deployed to production networks without<br>
requiring training nor instrumentation in the customer network.</li>
<li><em>Low overhead:</em> Once trained, the DRL agent can make routing<br>
decisions in only one step (≈ <em>ms</em> ), while its cost scales linearly<br>
with the network size.</li>
<li><em>Commercialization:</em> Network vendors can easily embed it in net-<br>
work devices or products, and successfully operate ‘‘arbitrary’’<br>
networks.</li>
</ul>
<p>We believe the combination of these features can enable the devel-<br>
opment of a new generation of networking solutions based on DRL that<br>
are more cost-effective than current approaches based on heuristics or<br>
linear optimization. All the topologies and scripts used in the experi-<br>
ments, as well as the source code of our DRL+GNN agent are publicly<br>
available [19].</p>
<p><strong>2. Background</strong></p>
<p>The solution proposed in this paper combines two machine learning<br>
mechanisms. First, we use a GNN to model computer network scenarios.<br>
GNNs are neural network architectures specifically designed to gen-<br>
eralize over graph-structured data [16]. In addition, they offer near<br>
real-time operation in the scale of milliseconds (see Section 6.2). Sec-<br>
ond, we use Deep Reinforcement Learning to build an agent that learns</p>
<p>(^1) Note that solutions based on transfer learning do not offer this property,<br>
as DRL agents need to be re-trained on the network where they finally operate.<br>
how to efficiently operate networks following a particular optimization<br>
goal. DRL applies the knowledge obtained in past optimizations to<br>
later decisions, without the necessity to run computationally intensive<br>
algorithms.<br>
<em>2.1. Graph Neural Networks</em><br>
Graph Neural Networks are a novel family of neural networks<br>
designed to operate over graphs. They were introduced in [17] and<br>
numerous variants have been developed since [20,21]. In their basic<br>
form, they consist of associating some initial states to the different<br>
elements of an input graph, and combining them considering how these<br>
elements are connected in the graph. An iterative algorithm updates<br>
the elements’ state and uses the resulting states to produce an output.<br>
The particularities of the problem to solve will determine which GNN<br>
variant is more suitable, depending on, for instance, the nature of the<br>
graph elements (i.e., nodes and edges) involved.<br>
Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNN) [18] are a well-known<br>
type of GNNs that apply an iterative message-passing algorithm to<br>
propagate information between the nodes of the graph. In a message-<br>
passing step, each node <em>k</em> receives messages from all the nodes in its<br>
neighborhood, denoted by <em>N(k)</em>. Messages are generated by applying a<br>
message function <em>m(</em> ⋅ <em>)</em> to the hidden states of node pairs in the graph.<br>
Then, they are combined by an aggregation function, for instance, a<br>
sum (Eq. (1)). Finally, an update function <em>u(</em> ⋅ <em>)</em> is used to compute a<br>
new hidden state for every node (Eq. (2)).<br>
<em>𝑀𝑡𝑘</em> +1=</p>
<h3>∑</h3>
<pre><code>𝑖 ∈ 𝑁 ( 𝑘 )
</code></pre>
<h3>𝑚 ( ℎ𝑡𝑘,ℎ𝑡𝑖 ) (1)</h3>
<h3>ℎ𝑡𝑘 +1= 𝑢 ( ℎ𝑡𝑘,𝑀𝑡𝑘 +1) (2)</h3>
<pre><code>Where functions m( ⋅ ) and u( ⋅ ) can be learned by neural networks.
After a certain number of iterations, the final node states are used by
a readout function r( ⋅ ) to produce an output for the given task. This
function can also be implemented by a neural network and is typically
tasked to predict properties of individual nodes (e.g., the node’s class)
or global properties of the graph.
GNNs have been able to achieve relevant performance results in
multiple domains where data is typically structured as a graph [18,22].
Since computer networks are fundamentally represented as graphs, it
is inherent in their design that GNNs offer unique advantages for net-
work modeling compared to traditional neural network architectures
(e.g., fully connected NN, Convolutional NN, etc.).
</code></pre>
<pre><code>2.2. Deep Reinforcement Learning
</code></pre>
<pre><code>DRL algorithms aim at learning a long-term strategy that leads to
maximize an objective function in an optimization problem. DRL agents
start from a tabula rasa state and they learn the optimal strategy by an
iterative process that explores the state and action spaces. These are
denoted by a set of states () and a set of actions (). Given a state s
∈, the agent will perform an action a ∈that produces a transition
to a new state s’ ∈, and will provide the agent with a reward r.
Then, the objective is to find a strategy that maximizes the cumulative
reward by the end of an episode. The definition of the end of an episode
depends on the optimization problem to address.
Q-learning [23] is a RL algorithm whose goal is to make an agent
learn a policy 𝜋 : →. The algorithm creates a table (a.k.a., q-
table) with all the possible combinations of states and actions. At the
beginning of the training, the table is initialized (e.g., with zeros or
random values) and during training, the agent updates these values
according to the rewards obtained after selecting an action. These
values, called q-values, represent the expected cumulative reward after
applying action a from state s , assuming that the agent follows the
current policy 𝜋 during the rest of the episode. During training, q-values
are updated using the Bellman equation (see Eq. (3)) where Q(𝑠𝑡,𝑎𝑡) is
the 𝑞 -value function at time-step t , 𝛼 is the learning rate, r(𝑠𝑡,𝑎𝑡) is the
</code></pre>
<p>reward obtained from selecting action <em>𝑎𝑡</em> from state <em>𝑠𝑡</em> and <em>𝛾</em> ∈ [0 <em>,</em> 1]is<br>
the discount factor.</p>
<p><em>𝑄</em> ( <em>𝑠𝑡,𝑎𝑡</em> ) = <em>𝑄</em> ( <em>𝑠𝑡,𝑎𝑡</em> ) + <em>𝛼</em></p>
<h3>(</h3>
<h3>𝑟 ( 𝑠𝑡,𝑎𝑡 )+</h3>
<pre><code>𝛾 max 𝑎 ′ 𝑄 ( 𝑠 ′ 𝑡,𝑎 ′) − 𝑄 ( 𝑠𝑡,𝑎𝑡 )
</code></pre>
<h3>) (3)</h3>
<p>Deep Q-Network (DQN) [24] is a more advanced algorithm based on<br>
Q-learning that uses a Deep Neural Network (DNN) to approximate the<br>
<em>𝑞</em> -value function. As the q-table size becomes larger, Q-learning faces<br>
difficulties to learn a policy from high dimensional state and action<br>
spaces. To overcome this problem, they proposed to use a DNN as a<br>
<em>𝑞</em> -value function estimator, relying on the generalization capabilities of<br>
DNNs to estimate the q-values of states and actions unseen in advance.<br>
For this reason, a NN well suited to understand and generalize over<br>
the input data of the DRL agent is crucial for the agents to perform<br>
well when facing states (or environments) never seen before. Addi-<br>
tionally, DQN uses an experience replay buffer to store past sequential<br>
experiences (i.e., stores tuples of <em>{s,a,r,s’}</em> ).</p>
<p><strong>3. Networkoptimizationscenario</strong></p>
<p>In this paper, we explore the potential of a GNN-based DRL agent<br>
to address the routing problem in Optical Transport Networks (OTN).<br>
Particularly, we consider a network scenario based on Software-Defined<br>
Networking, where the DRL agent (located in the control plane) has<br>
a global view of the current network state, and has to make routing<br>
decisions on every traffic demand as it arrives. We consider a traffic<br>
demand as the volume of traffic sent from a source to a destination<br>
node. This is a relevant optimization scenario that has been studied<br>
in the last decades in the optical networking community, where many<br>
solutions have been proposed [11,15,25].<br>
In our OTN scenario, the DRL agent makes routing decisions at<br>
the electrical domain, over a logical topology where nodes represent<br>
Reconfigurable Optical Add-Drop Multiplexers (ROADM) and edges are<br>
predefined lightpaths connecting them (see Fig. 1). The DRL agent<br>
receives traffic demands with different bandwidth requirements defined<br>
by the tuple{ <em>𝑠𝑟𝑐,𝑑𝑠𝑡,𝑏𝑎𝑛𝑑𝑤𝑖𝑑𝑡ℎ</em> }, and it has to select an end-to-end<br>
path for every demand. Particularly, end-to-end paths are defined as<br>
sequences of lightpaths connecting the source and destination of a<br>
demand. Since the agent operates at the electrical domain, traffic<br>
demands are defined as requests of Optical Data Units (ODUk), whose<br>
bandwidth requirements are defined in the ITU-T Recommendation<br>
G.709 [26]. The ODUk signals are then multiplexed into Optical Trans-<br>
port Units (OTUk), which are data frames including Forward Error<br>
Correction. Eventually, OTUk frames are mapped to different optical<br>
channels within the lightpaths of the topology.<br>
In this scenario, the routing problem is defined as finding the<br>
optimal routing policy for each incoming source–destination traffic<br>
demand. The learning process is guided by an objective function that<br>
aims to maximize the traffic volume allocated in the network in the<br>
long-term. We consider that a demand is properly allocated if there is<br>
enough available capacity in all the lightpaths forming the end-to-end<br>
path selected. Note that lightpaths are the edges in the logical topology<br>
where the agent operates. The demands do not expire, occupying the<br>
lightpaths until the end of a DRL episode. This implies a challenging<br>
task for the agent, since it has not only to identify critical resources<br>
on networks (e.g., potential bottlenecks), but also to deal with the<br>
uncertainty in the generation of future traffic demands. The following<br>
constraints summarize the traffic demand routing problem in the OTN<br>
scenario:</p>
<ul>
<li>The agent must make sequential routing decisions for every in-<br>
coming traffic demand</li>
<li>Traffic demands cannot be split over multiple paths</li>
<li>Previous traffic demands cannot be rerouted and they occupy the<br>
links’ capacities until the end of the episode</li>
</ul>
<pre><code>Fig. 1. Schematic representation of the DRL agent in the OTN routing scenario.
</code></pre>
<pre><code>The optimal solution to the OTN optimization problem can be found
by solving its Markov Decision Process (MDP) [27]. To do this, we
can use techniques such as Dynamic Programming, which consist of
an iterative process over all MDP’s states until convergence. The MDP
for the traffic demand allocation problem consists of all the possible
network topology states and the transition probabilities between states.
Notice that in our scenario we have uniform transition probabilities
from one state to the next. One limitation of solving MDPs optimally is
that it becomes infeasible for large and complex optimization problems.
As the problem size grows, so does the MDP’s state space, where the
space complexity (in number of states) is 𝑆 ≈ 𝑂 ( 𝑁 E), having 𝑁 as the
number of different capacities a link can have and 𝐸 as the number of
links. Therefore, to solve the MDP the algorithm will spend more time
on iterating over all MDP’s states.
</code></pre>
<p><strong>4. GNN-basedDRLagentdesign</strong></p>
<pre><code>In this section, we describe the DRL+GNN architecture proposed
in this paper. On one side, we have the GNN-based DRL agent which
defines the actions to apply on the network topology. These actions con-
sist of allocating the demands on one of the candidate paths. Our DRL
agent implements the DQN algorithm [24], where the 𝑞 -value function
is modeled by a GNN. On the other side, we have an environment which
defines the optimization problem to solve. This environment stores
the network topology, together with the link features. In addition, the
environment is responsible of generating the reward once an action is
performed, which will indicate the agent if that action was good or not.
The learning process is based on an iterative process, where at
each time step, the agent receives a graph-structured network state
observation from the environment. Then, the GNN constructs a graph
representation where the links of the topology are the graph entities.
In this representation, the link hidden states are initialized consider-
ing the input link-level features and the routing action to evaluate
(see more details in Sections 4.1–4.3). With this representation, an
iterative message passing algorithm runs between the links’ hidden
states according to the graph structure. The output of this algorithm
(i.e., new links hidden states) is aggregated into a global hidden state,
that encodes topology information, and then is processed by a DNN.
This process makes the GNN topology invariant because the global
hidden state length is pre-defined and it will always have the same
length for different topology sizes. At the end of the message passing
phase, the GNN outputs a 𝑞 -value estimate. This 𝑞 -value is evaluated
over a limited set of actions, and finally the DRL agent selects the action
with highest 𝑞 -value.
</code></pre>
<pre><code>4.1. Environment
</code></pre>
<pre><code>The network state is defined by the topology links’ features, which
include the link capacity and link betweenness. The former indicates
</code></pre>
<pre><code>Fig. 2. Action representation in the link hidden states.
</code></pre>
<p>the amount of capacity available on the link. The latter is a measure of<br>
centrality inherited from graph theory that indicates how many paths<br>
may potentially traverse the link. From the experimental results we<br>
observed that this feature helps reduce the grid search of the hyperpa-<br>
rameter tuning for the DRL agent. This is because the betweenness helps<br>
the agent converge faster to a good policy. In particular, we compute<br>
the link betweenness in the following way: for each pair of nodes in<br>
the topology, we compute <em>𝑘</em> candidate paths (e.g., the <em>𝑘</em> shortest paths),<br>
and we maintain a per-link counter that indicates how many paths pass<br>
through the link. Thus, the betweenness on each link is the number of<br>
end-to-end paths crossing the link divided by the total number of paths.</p>
<p><em>4.2. Action space</em></p>
<p>In this section we describe how the routing actions are represented<br>
in the DRL+GNN agent. Note that the number of possible routing<br>
combinations for each source–destination node pair typically results in<br>
a high dimensional action space in large-scale real-world networks. This<br>
makes the routing problem complex for the DRL agent, since it should<br>
estimate the q-values for all the possible actions to apply (i.e., routing<br>
configurations). To overcome this problem, the action space must<br>
be carefully designed to reduce the dimensionality. In addition, to<br>
enable generalization to other topologies, the action space should be<br>
equivalent across topologies. In other words, if the actions in the<br>
training topology are represented by shortest paths, in the evaluation<br>
topology they should also be shortest paths. If the action space would<br>
be different (e.g., multiple paths between a source–destination node<br>
pair), the agent will have problems learning and it will not generalize<br>
well. To leverage the generalization capability of GNN, we introduced<br>
the action into the agent in the form of a graph. This makes the action<br>
representation invariant to node and edge permutation, which means<br>
that, once the GNN is successfully trained, it is able to understand<br>
actions over arbitrary graph structures (i.e., over different network<br>
states and topologies).<br>
Considering the above, we limit the action set to <em>k</em> candidate paths<br>
for each source–destination node pair. To maintain a good trade-off<br>
between flexibility to route traffic and the cost to evaluate all the<br>
possible actions, we selected a set with the <em>k=4</em> shortest paths (by<br>
number of hops) for each source–destination node pair. This follows<br>
a criteria originally proposed by [15]. Note that the action set differs<br>
depending on the source and destination nodes of the traffic demand<br>
to be routed.<br>
To represent the action, we introduce it within the network state.<br>
Particularly, we consider an additional link-level feature, which is the<br>
bandwidth allocated over the link after applying the routing action.<br>
This value corresponds to the bandwidth demand of the current traffic<br>
request to be allocated. Likewise, the links that are not included in<br>
the path selected by the action will have this feature set to zero.</p>
<pre><code>Table 1
Input features of the link hidden states. N corresponds
to the size of the hidden state vector.
Notation Description
𝑥 1 Link available capacity
𝑥 2 Link betweenness
𝑥 3 Action vector (bandwidth allocated)
𝑥 4 − 𝑥𝑁 Zero padding
</code></pre>
<pre><code>Since our OTN environment has a limited number of traffic requests
with various discrete bandwidth demands, we represent the bandwidth
allocated with a N -element one-hot encoding vector, where N is the
vector length.
Fig. 2 illustrates the representation of the action in the hidden state
of the links in a simple network scenario. A traffic request from node
1 to node 5, with a traffic demand of 8 bandwidth units, is allocated
over the path formed by the nodes {1,2,3,5}. To summarize, Table 1
provides a description of the features included in the links’ hidden
states. These values represent both the network state and the action,
which is the input needed to model the 𝑞 -value function 𝑄 ( 𝑠,𝑎 ).
The size of the hidden states is typically larger than the number
of features in the hidden states. This is to enable each link to store
information of himself (i.e., his own initial features) plus the aggregated
information coming from all the links’ neighbors (see Section 4.3). If the
hidden state size is equal to the number of link features, the links will
not have space to store information about the neighboring links without
losing information. This results in a poor graph embedding after the
readout function. On the contrary, if the state size is very large, it can
lead to a large GNN model, which can overfit to the data. A common
approach is to set the state size larger than the number of features and
to fill the vector with zeros.
</code></pre>
<pre><code>4.3. GNN architecture
</code></pre>
<pre><code>The GNN model is based on the Message Passing Neural Net-
work [18] model. In our case, we consider the link entity and perform
the message passing process between all links. We choose link entities,
instead of node entities, because the link features are what define the
OTN routing optimization problem. Node entities could be added when
addressing an optimization problem that needs to incorporate node-
level features (e.g., I/O buffer size, scheduling algorithm). Algorithm
1 shows a formal description of the message passing process where the
algorithm receives as input the links’ features ( 𝑥𝑙 ) and outputs a 𝑞 -value
( q ).
The algorithm performs T message passing steps. A graphical rep-
resentation can be seen in Fig. 3, where the algorithm iterates over all
</code></pre>
<pre><code>Fig. 3. Message passing architecture.
</code></pre>
<p><strong>Algorithm1</strong> Message Passing</p>
<pre><code>Input ∶ 𝐱 𝑙
Output ∶ 𝐡 𝑇𝑙,𝑞
1: foreach 𝑙 ∈ do
2: ℎ^0 𝑙 ←[ 𝐱 𝑙, 0 ... , 0]
3: for 𝑡 = 1to 𝑇 do
4: foreach 𝑙 ∈ do
5: 𝑀𝑙𝑡 +1=
</code></pre>
<h3>∑</h3>
<h3>𝑖 ∈ 𝑁 ( 𝑙 ) 𝑚</h3>
<h3>(</h3>
<h3>ℎ𝑡𝑙,ℎ𝑡𝑖</h3>
<h3>)</h3>
<h3>6: ℎ𝑡𝑙 +1= 𝑢</h3>
<h3>(</h3>
<h3>ℎ𝑡𝑙,𝑀𝑙𝑡 +</h3>
<h3>)</h3>
<h3>7: 𝑟𝑑𝑡 ←∑ 𝑙 ∈ ℎ𝑙</h3>
<pre><code>8: 𝑞 ← 𝑅 ( rdt )
</code></pre>
<p>links of the network topology. For each link, its features are combined<br>
with those of the neighboring links using a fully-connected, correspond-<br>
ing to M in Fig. 3. The outputs of these operations are called <em>messages</em><br>
according to the GNN notation. Then, the <em>messages</em> computed for each<br>
link with their neighbors are aggregated using an element-wise sum<br>
(line 5 in Algorithm 1). Afterwards, a Recurrent NN (RNN) is used to<br>
update the link hidden states <em>ℎ𝐿𝐾</em> with the new aggregated information<br>
(line 6 in Algorithm 1). At the end of the message passing phase, the<br>
resulting link states are aggregated using an element-wise sum (line<br>
7 in Algorithm 1). The result is passed through a fully-connected DNN<br>
which models the readout function of the GNN. The output of this latter<br>
function is the estimated <em>𝑞</em> -value of the input state and action.<br>
The role of the RNN is to learn how the link states change along the<br>
message passing phase. As the link information is being spread through<br>
the graph, each hidden state will store information from links that are<br>
farther and farther apart. Therefore, the concept of time appears. RNNs<br>
are a NN architecture that are tailored to capture sequential behavior<br>
(e.g., text, video, time-series). In addition, some RNN architectures<br>
(e.g., GRU) are designed to process large sequences (e.g., long text<br>
sentences in NLP). Specifically, they internally contain gates that are<br>
designed to mitigate the vanishing gradients, a common problem with<br>
large sequences [28]. This makes RNNs suitable to learn how the links’<br>
state evolve during the message passing phase, even for large <em>T</em>.</p>
<p><em>4.4. DRL agent operation</em></p>
<p>The DRL agent operates by interacting with the environment. In<br>
Algorithm 2 we can observe a pseudocode describing the DRL agent<br>
operation. At the beginning, we initialize the environment <em>env</em> by<br>
initializing all the link features. At the same time, the environment<br>
generates a traffic demand to be allocated by the tuple{ <em>𝑠𝑟𝑐,𝑑𝑠𝑡,𝑏𝑤</em> }<br>
and an environment state <em>s</em>. We also initialize the cumulative reward<br>
to zero, define the action set size and create the experience replay<br>
buffer ( <em>agt.mem</em> ). Afterwards, we execute a while loop (lines 3–16) that<br>
finishes when there is some demand that cannot be allocated in the<br>
network topology. For each of the <em>k=4</em> shortest paths, we allocate the<br>
demand along all the links forming the path and compute a <em>𝑞</em> -value<br>
(lines 7–9). Once we have the <em>𝑞</em> -value for each state-action pair, the<br>
next action <em>a</em> to apply is selected using an <em>𝜖</em> -greedy exploration strategy<br>
(line 10) [24]. The action is then applied to the environment, leading<br>
to a new state <em>s’</em> , a reward <em>r</em> and a flag <em>Done</em> indicating if there is some<br>
link without enough capacity to support the demand. Additionally, the<br>
environment returns a new traffic demand tuple{ <em>𝑠𝑟𝑐</em> ′ <em>,𝑑𝑠𝑡</em> ′ <em>,𝑏𝑤</em> ′}. The<br>
information about the state transition is stored in the experience replay<br>
buffer (line 13). This information will be used later on to train the GNN<br>
in the <em>agt.replay()</em> call (line 15), which is executed every <em>𝑀</em> training<br>
iterations.</p>
<pre><code>Algorithm2 DRL Agent operation
1: 𝑠,𝑠𝑟𝑐,𝑑𝑠𝑡,𝑏𝑤 ← env.init_env()
2: 𝑟𝑒𝑤𝑎𝑟𝑑 ← 0 , 𝑘 ← 4 , 𝑎𝑔𝑡.𝑚𝑒𝑚 ← { } , 𝐷𝑜𝑛𝑒 ← False
3: while not Done do
4: 𝑘 _ 𝑞 _ 𝑣𝑎𝑙𝑢𝑒𝑠 ← { }
5: 𝑘 _ 𝑠ℎ𝑜𝑟𝑡𝑒𝑠𝑡 _ 𝑝𝑎𝑡ℎ𝑠 ← compute_k_paths(k, src, dst)
6: for i in 0 ,...,𝑘 do
7: 𝑝 ′← get_path(i, k_shortest_paths)
8: 𝑠 ′← env.alloc_demand(s, p’, src, dst, dem)
9: 𝑘 _ 𝑞 _ 𝑣𝑎𝑙𝑢𝑒𝑠 [ 𝑖 ]← compute_q_value(s’, p’)
10: 𝑞 _ 𝑣𝑎𝑙𝑢𝑒 ← epsilon_greedy(k_q_values,𝜖)
11: 𝑎 ← get_action(q_value, k_shortest_paths, s)
12: 𝑟,𝐷𝑜𝑛𝑒,𝑠 ′ ,𝑠𝑟𝑐 ′ ,𝑑𝑠𝑡 ′ ,𝑏𝑤 ′← env.step(s, a)
13: 𝑎𝑔𝑡.𝑟𝑚𝑏 ( 𝑠,𝑠𝑟𝑐,𝑑𝑠𝑡,𝑏𝑤,𝑎,𝑟,𝑠 ′ ,𝑠𝑟𝑐 ′ ,𝑑𝑠𝑡 ′ ,𝑏𝑤 ′)
14: 𝑟𝑒𝑤𝑎𝑟𝑑 ← 𝑟𝑒𝑤𝑎𝑟𝑑 + 𝑟
15: If training_steps % M == 0: agt.replay()
16: 𝑠𝑟𝑐 ← src’ ; 𝑑𝑠𝑡 ← dst’ ; 𝑏𝑤 ← bw’,𝑠 ← s’
</code></pre>
<p><strong>5. Experimentalresults</strong></p>
<pre><code>In this section we evaluate our GNN-based DRL agent to optimize
the routing configuration in the OTN scenario described in Section 3.
Particularly, the experiments in this section are focused on evaluat-
ing the performance and generalization capabilities of the proposed
DRL+GNN agent. Afterwards, in Section 6, we analyze the scalability
properties of our solution and discuss other relevant aspects related to
the deployment on production networks.
</code></pre>
<pre><code>5.1. Evaluation setup
</code></pre>
<pre><code>We implemented the DRL+GNN solution described in Section 4
with Tensorflow [29] and evaluated it on an OTN network simula-
tor implemented using the OpenAI Gym framework [30]. The source
code, together with all the training and evaluation results are publicly
available [19].
In the OTN simulator, we consider three traffic demand types
(ODU2, ODU3, and ODU4), whose bandwidth requirements are ex-
pressed in terms of multiples of ODU0 signals (i.e., 8, 32, and 64 ODU
bandwidth units respectively) [26]. When the DRL agent correctly allo-
cates a demand, it receives an immediate reward being the bandwidth
of the current traffic demand if it was properly allocated, otherwise the
reward is 0. We consider that a demand is successfully allocated if all
the links in the path selected by the DRL agent have enough available
capacity to carry such demand. Likewise, episodes end when a traffic
demand was not correctly allocated. Traffic demands are generated by
uniformly selecting a source–destination node pair and a traffic demand
type (ODUk). This makes the problem even more difficult for the DRL
agent, since the uniform traffic distribution hinders the exploitation of
prediction systems to anticipate possible demands difficult to allocate.
In other words, all traffic demands are equally probable to appear in
the future, making it more difficult for the DRL agent to estimate the
expected future rewards.
Initial experiments were carried out to choose an appropriate gradi-
ent-based optimization algorithm and to find the hyperparameter val-
ues for the DRL+GNN agent. For the GNN model, we defined the
links’ hidden states ℎ𝑙 as 27-element vectors (filled with the features
described in Table 1). Note that the size of the hidden states is related to
the amount of information they may potentially encode. Larger network
topologies and complex network optimization scenarios might need
larger sizes for the hidden state vectors. In every forward propagation
of the GNN we execute T=7 message passing steps using batches of 32
samples. The optimizer used is the Stochastic Gradient Descent [31]
with a learning rate of 10−^4 and a momentum of 0.9. We start the
</code></pre>
<p><em>𝜖</em> -greedy exploration strategy with <em>𝜖</em> =1.0 and maintain this value dur-<br>
ing 70 training iterations. Afterwards, <em>𝜖</em> decays exponentially every<br>
episode. The experience buffer stores 4000 samples and is implemented<br>
as a FIFO queue (first in, first out). We applied <em>l2</em> regularization and<br>
dropout to the readout function with a coefficient of 0.1 in both cases.<br>
The discount factor <em>𝛾</em> was set to 0.95.</p>
<p><em>5.2. Methodology</em></p>
<p>We divided the evaluation of our DRL+GNN agent in two sets of<br>
experiments. In the first set, we focused on reasoning about the perfor-<br>
mance and generalization capabilities of our solution. For illustration<br>
purposes, we chose two particular network scenarios and analyzed<br>
them extensively. As a baseline, we implemented the DRL-based system<br>
proposed in [15], a state-of-the-art solution for routing optimization in<br>
OTNs. Later on, in Section 6, we evaluated our solution on real-world<br>
network topologies and analyzed its scalability in terms of computation<br>
time and generalization capabilities.<br>
To find the <em>optimal</em> MDP solution to the OTN optimization problem<br>
is infeasible due to its complexity. Take as an example a small network<br>
topology with 6 nodes and 8 edges, where the links have capacities of<br>
3 ODU0 units, there is only one bandwidth type available (1 ODU0)<br>
and there are 4 possible actions. The resulting number of states of the<br>
MDP is 5^8 * 6 * 5 *1 ≈ 1_._ 17 <em>𝑒</em> 7. To find a solution to the MDP we can use<br>
Dynamic Programming algorithms such as value iteration. However,<br>
this algorithm has a time complexity to solve the MDP of <em>𝑂</em> ( <em>𝑆</em>^2 <em>𝐴</em> ),<br>
where <em>𝑆</em> and <em>𝐴</em> are the number of states and actions respectively and<br>
<em>𝑆</em> ≈ <em>𝑂</em> ( <em>𝑁</em> E), having <em>𝑁</em> as the number of different capacities a link can<br>
have and E as the number of links.<br>
As an alternative, we compare the DRL+GNN agent performance<br>
with a theoretical fluid model (labeled as <em>Theoretical Fluid</em> ). This model<br>
is a theoretical approach which considers that traffic demands may<br>
be split into the <em>k=4</em> candidate paths proportionally to the available<br>
capacity they have. This routing policy is aimed at avoiding congestion<br>
on links. For instance, paths with low available capacity will carry a<br>
small proportion of the traffic volume from new demands. Note that<br>
this model is non-realizable because ODU demands cannot be split in<br>
real OTN scenarios. However, this model is fast to compute and serves<br>
us as a reference to compare the performance of the DRL+GNN agent.<br>
In addition, we also use a load balancing routing policy (LB), which<br>
selects uniformly random one path among the <em>k=4</em> candidate shortest<br>
paths to allocate the traffic demand.<br>
We trained the DRL+GNN agent in an OTN routing scenario on<br>
the 14-node Nsfnet topology [32], where we considered that the links<br>
represent lightpaths with capacity for 200 ODU0 signals. Note that<br>
the capacity is shared on both directions of the links and that the<br>
bandwidth of different traffic demands is expressed in multiples of<br>
ODU0 signals (i.e., 8, 32 or 64 ODU0 bandwidth units). We ran 1000<br>
training iterations where the agent received traffic demands and allo-<br>
cated them on one of the <em>k=4</em> shortest paths available in the action set.<br>
The model with highest performance was selected to be benchmarked<br>
against traditional routing optimization strategies and state-of-the-art<br>
DRL-based solutions.</p>
<p><em>5.3. Performance evaluation against state-of-the-art DRL-based solutions</em></p>
<p>In this evaluation experiment, we compare our DRL+GNN agent<br>
against state-of-the-art DRL-based solutions. Particularly, we adapted<br>
the solution proposed in [15] to operate in scenarios where links share<br>
their capacity in both directions. We trained two different instances<br>
of the state-of-the-art DRL agent in two network scenarios: the 14-<br>
node Nsfnet and the 24-node Geant2 topology [33]. We made 1000<br>
experiments with uniform traffic generation to provide representative<br>
results. Note that both, the proposed DRL+GNN agent and the state-<br>
of-the-art DRL solution, were evaluated over the same list of generated<br>
demands.</p>
<pre><code>We run two experiments to compare the performance of our DRL+
GNN with the results obtained by the state-of-the-art DRL (SoA DRL).
In the first experiment, we evaluated the DRL+GNN agent against
the SoA DRL agent trained on Nsfnet, the LB routing policy, and the
theoretical fluid model. We evaluated the four routing strategies on
the Nsfnet topology and compared their performance. In Fig. 4(a), we
can observe a bloxplot with the evaluation results of 1000 evaluation
experiments. The 𝑦 -axis indicates the agent score, which corresponds to
the bandwidth allocated by the agent. Fig. 4(c) shows the Cumulative
Distribution Function (CDF) of the relative score obtained with respect
to the fluid model. In this experiment we could also observe that the
proposed DRL+GNN agent slightly outperforms the SoA DRL-based
by allocating 6.6% more bandwidth. In the second experiment, we
evaluated the same models (DRL+GNN, SoA DRL, LB, and Theoretical
Fluid) on the Geant2 topology, but in this case the SoA DRL agent
was trained on Geant2. The resulting boxplot can be seen in Fig. 4(b)
and the CDF of the evaluation samples in Fig. 4(d). Similarly, in this
case our agent performs slightly better than the SoA DRL approach (3%
more bandwidth).
We run another experiment to compare the generalization capabil-
ities of our DRL+GNN agent. In this experiment, we evaluated the
DRL+GNN agent (trained on Nsfnet) against the SoA DRL agent trained
on Nsfnet, and evaluated both agents on the Geant2 topology. The
resulting boxplot can be seen in Fig. 5(a) and the corresponding CDF in
Fig. 5(b). The results indicate that in this scenario the DRL+GNN agent
also outperforms the SoA DRL agent. In this case, in 80% of the experi-
ments our DRL+GNN agent achieved more than 45% of performance
improvement with respect to the SoA DRL proposal. These results
show that while the proposed DRL+GNN agent is able to generalize
and achieve outstanding performance in the unseen Geant2 topology
(Figs. 5(a) and 5(b)), the SoA DRL agent performs poorly when applied
to topologies not seen during training. This reveals the lack of gener-
alization capability of the latter DRL-based solution compared to the
agent proposed in this paper.
</code></pre>
<pre><code>5.4. Use case: Link failure resilience
</code></pre>
<pre><code>This subsection presents a use case where we evaluate if our
DRL+GNN agent can adapt successfully to changes in the network
topology. For this, we consider the case of a network with link failures.
Previous work showed that real-world network topologies change dur-
ing time (e.g., due to link failures) [1,34,35]. These changes in network
connectivity are unpredictable and they have a significant impact
in protocol convergence [34] or on fulfilling network optimization
goals [1].
In this evaluation, we considered a range of scenarios that can
experience up to 10 link failures. Thus, the DRL+GNN agent is tasked
to find new routing configurations that avoid the affected links while
still maximizing the total bandwidth allocated. We executed experi-
ments where 𝑛 ∈ [1 , 10]links are randomly removed from the Geant
topology. We compare the score (i.e., bandwidth allocated) achieved
by the DRL+GNN agent with respect to the theoretical fluid model.
Fig. 6(a) shows the average score over 1000 experiments (y-axis) as a
function of the number of link failures (x-axis). There, we can observe
that the DRL+GNN agent can maintain better performance than the
theoretical baseline even in the extreme case of 10 concurrent link
failures. Likewise, Fig. 6(b) shows the relative score of our DRL+GNN
agent against the theoretical fluid model. In line with the previous
results, the relative score is maintained as links are removed from the
topology. This suggests that the proposed DRL+GNN architecture is
able to adapt to topology changes.
</code></pre>
<pre><code>Fig. 4. Performance evaluation against state-of-the-art DRL. Notice that the vertical lines in 4(c) and 4(d) indicate the same performance as the theoretical fluid model.
</code></pre>
<pre><code>Fig. 5. Evaluation on Geant2 of DRL-based solutions trained on Nsfnet.
</code></pre>
<p><strong>6. Discussionondeployment</strong></p>
<p>In this section we analyze and discuss relevant aspects of our<br>
DRL+GNN architecture towards deployment in production networks.<br>
In the context of self-driving networks, DRL cannot succeed without<br>
generalization capabilities. Training a DRL agent requires instrument-<br>
ing the network with configurations that may disrupt the service. As<br>
a result, training in the customer’s network may be unfeasible. With<br>
generalization capabilities, the DRL agent can be trained in a controlled<br>
lab (for instance at the vendor’s facilities) and shipped to the customer.<br>
Once deployed, it can operate efficiently in an unseen network or</p>
<pre><code>scenario. Fig. 7 illustrates this training and deployment process of a
product based on our DRL+GNN architecture.
To better understand the technical feasibility and scalability proper-
ties of such a product in terms of cost and generalization, we designed
two experiments. First, we analyze how the effectiveness of our agent
scales with the network size, by training it in a single (small) network
and evaluating its performance in synthetic and real-world network
topologies. Second, we analyze the scalability of our agent in terms of
computation time after deployment (i.e., the time it takes for the agent
to make routing decisions). This is particularly relevant in real-time
networking scenarios.
</code></pre>
<pre><code>Fig. 6. DRL+GNN evaluation on a use case with link failures.
</code></pre>
<pre><code>Fig. 7. DRL+GNN deployment process overview by incorporating it into a product.
</code></pre>
<p><em>6.1. Generalization over network topologies</em></p>
<p>In both scenarios we used the DRL+GNN agent trained in a <em>single</em><br>
topology (14 nodes Nsfnet) and we analyzed its performance in larger<br>
topologies (up to 100 nodes) not seen during training.</p>
<p><em>6.1.1. Synthetic topologies</em><br>
In this experiment we generated a total of 180 synthetic topologies<br>
with an increasing number of nodes. For each topology size – in number<br>
of nodes – we generated 20 topologies and we evaluated the agent on<br>
1000 episodes. To do this, we used the NetworkX python library [36] to<br>
generate random network topologies between 20 and 100 nodes with<br>
similar <em>average node degree</em> to Nsfnet. This allows us to analyze how the<br>
network size affects the performance.<br>
Fig. 8(a) shows how the performance scales inversely with the<br>
topology size. For benchmark purposes, we computed the relative<br>
score with respect to the theoretical fluid model. The agent shows<br>
a remarkable performance in unseen topologies. As an example, the<br>
agent has a similar performance to the theoretical fluid model in<br>
the 30-node topologies, which double the size of the single 14-node<br>
topology seen during training. In addition, in the 100-node topologies,<br>
we observe only a 15% drop in performance. This result shows that<br>
the generalization properties of our solution degrade gracefully with<br>
the size of the network. It is well-known that deep learning models<br>
lose generalization capability as the distribution of the data seen during<br>
training differs from the evaluation samples (see Section 6.3).</p>
<p><em>6.1.2. Real-world network topologies</em><br>
In this section we evaluate the generalization capabilities of our<br>
DRL+GNN agent, trained in Nsfnet, on 232 real-world topologies ob-<br>
tained from the Topology Zoo [2] dataset. Specifically, we take all</p>
<pre><code>Table 2
Real-world topology features (minimum and maximum values).
Feature Minimum Maximum
Num. nodes 6 92
Num. edges 5 101
Avg. node degree 1.667 8
Var. node degree 0.001 41.
Diameter 1 31
</code></pre>
<pre><code>the topologies that have up to 100 nodes. In Table 2 we can see the
features extracted from the resulting topologies. The diameter feature
corresponds to the maximum eccentricity (i.e., maximum distance from
one node to another node). The ranges of the different topology fea-
tures indicate that our topology dataset contains different topology
distributions.
We executed 1000 evaluation episodes and computed the average
reward achieved by the DRL+GNN agents, the LB, and the theoretical
fluid routing strategies for each topology. Then, we computed the
relative performance (in %) of our agent and the LB policy with respect
to the theoretical fluid model. Fig. 8(b) shows the results where, for
readability, we sort the topologies according to the difference of score
between the DRL+GNN agent and the LB policy. In the left side of
the figure we observe some topology samples where the scores of all
three routing strategies coincide. This kind of behavior is normal in
topologies where for each input traffic demand, there are not many
paths to route the traffic demand (e.g., in ring or star topologies). As
the number of paths increases, routing optimization becomes necessary
to maximize the number of traffic demands allocated.
We also trained a DRL+GNN agent only in the Geant2 topology. The
mean relative score (with respect to the theoretical fluid) of evaluating
the model on all real-world topologies was+4.78%. In the interest of
space, we omit this figure. These results indicate that our DRL+GNN
architecture generalizes well to topologies never seen during training,
independently of the topology used during training.
These experiments show the robustness of our architecture to oper-
ate in real-world topologies that largely differ from the scenarios seen
during training. Even when trained in a single 14-node topology, the
agent achieves good performance in topologies of up to 100 nodes.
</code></pre>
<pre><code>6.2. Computation time
</code></pre>
<pre><code>In this section we analyze the computation time of an already
trained DRL+GNN agent when deployed in a realistic scenario. For
this purpose, we used the synthetic topologies generated before in
Section 6.1.1, and we executed 1000 episodes for each one and we
measured the computation time. This is the time the agent takes to
select the best path to allocate all the incoming traffic requests. For
</code></pre>
<pre><code>Fig. 8. DRL+GNN relative performance with respect to the fluid model over 180 synthetic topologies (a) and 232 real-world topologies (b).
</code></pre>
<p><strong>Fig. 9.</strong> DRL+GNN average computation time (in milliseconds) over different topology<br>
sizes.</p>
<p>this experiment we used <em>off-the-shelf</em> hardware without any specific<br>
hardware accelerator (64-bit Ubuntu 16.04 LTS with processor Intel<br>
Core i5-8400 with 2.80 GHz×6 cores and 8 GB of RAM memory).<br>
Results should be understood only as a reference to analyze the scal-<br>
ability properties of our solution. Real implementations in a network<br>
device would be highly optimized.<br>
Fig. 9 shows the computation time for all episodes. The dots cor-<br>
respond to the average agent operation time over all the episodes<br>
and the confidence interval corresponds to the 5/95 percentiles. The<br>
execution time is in the order of few <em>ms</em> and grows linearly with the<br>
size of the topology. This is expected due to the way the message-<br>
passing in the GNN has been designed. The results indicate that, in<br>
terms of deployment, the proposed DRL+GNN agent has interesting<br>
features. It is capable of optimizing unseen networks achieving good<br>
performance, as optimization algorithms, but in one single step and in<br>
tens of milliseconds, as heuristics.</p>
<p><em>6.3. Discussion</em></p>
<p>In this paper we propose a data-driven solution to solve a routing<br>
problem in OTN. This means that our DRL agent learns from data that is<br>
obtained from past interactions with the environment. This method has<br>
the main limitation that when evaluated on out-of-distribution data, its<br>
performance is expected to drop. In our scenario, out-of-distribution is<br>
any data related to network topology, link features and traffic matrix<br>
that is radically different from the data seen during the training process.<br>
The experimental results on synthetic and real-world topologies<br>
(Section 6.1.1 and Section 6.1.2 respectively) show that the DRL+GNN<br>
architecture has performance issues on some topologies. This perfor-<br>
mance drop is related to the diverging network characteristics from the<br>
topology used during training. The link features are normalized and</p>

<pre><code>the traffic demands always have the same bandwidth values, which
excludes them as the source of the performance drop. However, the
network topology changes, which has a direct impact on the DRL agent
performance.
Table 3 shows different topology metrics for each topology size (in
number of nodes). The edge betweenness is computed in the following
way: for each edge compute the sum of the fraction of all-pairs of
shortest paths that pass through the edge, and then make the mean
of all edges. This applies in a similar way to the node betweenness. In
addition, the DRL+GNN’s performance with respect to the Theoretical
Fluid model is also shown. The values correspond to the means from
evaluating on all network topologies for each topology size (i.e., the
means from the results in Fig. 8(a)).
Even though the synthetic topologies were generated in a way to
have a similar node degree like Nsfnet, we can see that other metrics
diverge as the topologies become larger. Specifically, the node and
edge betweenness become smaller, which indicates that the pairs of
</code></pre>
<p>shortest paths are more distributed. In other words, for small topologies<br>
the nodes and edges have proportionally more shortest paths crossing<br>
them than for larger ones. The network metrics clearly indicate that the<br>
more different the topologies are than Nsfnet, the worse is the DRL’s<br>
performance.<br>
Table 4 shows a similar table but for the real-world topologies. In<br>
this case, the performance results correspond to the means from the<br>
results in Fig. 8(b). Following a similar reasoning, we can see that<br>
the real-world topologies where the DRL+GNN architecture achieves<br>
the worst performance are radically different from Nsfnet (i.e., top left<br>
topologies from Fig. 8(b)). In addition, we visualized the topologies<br>
with id 0, 1 and 2 and observed that they correspond to topologies<br>
that have some nodes with a very high connectivity (see the variance<br>
of the node degree in Table 4). Similarly to the synthetic topologies, we<br>
again observe that the more different the topologies are than Nsfnet, the<br>
worse is the DRL+GNN’s performance.<br>
There are several things that could be done to improve the gener-<br>
alization capabilities for such topologies. A straightforward approach<br>
would be to incorporate topologies with different characteristics to the<br>
training set. In addition, the DRL+GNN architecture could be improved<br>
using fine-tuned traditional Deep Learning techniques (e.g., regulariza-<br>
tion, dropout). Finally, the work from [37] suggests that aggregating<br>
the information of the neighboring links using a combination of mean,<br>
min, max, and sum of the links’ states improves generalization. We<br>
consider that improving the generalization is outside the scope of our<br>
work and we left it as future work.</p>
<p><strong>7. Relatedwork</strong></p>
<p>Network optimization is a well-known and established topic whose<br>
fundamental goal is to operate networks efficiently. Most of the works<br>
in the literature use traditional methods to optimize a network state<br>
based on Integer Linear Programming (ILP) or Constraint Programming<br>
(CP). A relevant work is [1] where they convert high-level goals, indi-<br>
cated by the network operator, into valid network configurations using<br>
constraint programming. The authors from [38] propose a solution<br>
based on ILP for multicast routing in OTN. In addition, they propose<br>
to use a heuristic based on genetic algorithms to improve the locally<br>
optimal solution and to reduce the computational complexity. Similar<br>
work using genetic algorithms are [39,40].<br>
To find the optimal routing configuration from a given traffic matrix<br>
is a fundamental problem, which has been demonstrated to be NP-<br>
hard [41,42]. In this context, several DRL-based solutions have been<br>
proposed to address routing optimization. In [11] they propose a DRL<br>
solution for spectrum assignment using Q-learning and convolutional<br>
NNs. Similarly, in [15] they propose a more elaborated representation<br>
of the network state to help the DRL agent capture easily the singular-<br>
ities of the network topology. In [43] they propose a scalable method<br>
to solve Traffic Engineering problems with DRL in large networks. The<br>
work from [44] combines DRL with Linear Programming to minimize<br>
the utilization of the most congested link. In [45] they propose and<br>
compare different DRL-based algorithms to solve a Traffic Engineering<br>
problem in SD-WAN.<br>
However, most of the proposed DRL-based solutions fail to gener-<br>
alize to unseen scenarios. This is because they pre-process the data<br>
from the network state and present it in the form of fixed-size matrices<br>
(e.g., adjacency matrix of a network topology). Then, these repre-<br>
sentations are processed by traditional neural network architectures<br>
(e.g., fully connected, convolutional neural networks). These neural<br>
architectures are not suited to learn and generalize over data that is<br>
inherently structured as a graph. Consequently, state-of-the-art DRL<br>
agents perform poorly when they are evaluated in different topologies<br>
that were not seen during the training.<br>
There have been several attempts to use GNN in the communication<br>
networks field. In [46] they use GNN to learn shortest-path routing<br>
and max–min routing in a supervised learning approach. In [47] they</p>
<pre><code>combine GNN with DRL to solve a network planning problem. Another
relevant work is the one from [48] where they use a distributed setup
of DRL agents to solve a Traffic Engineering problem in a decentralized
way. The work from [10] proposes to use GNN to predict network
metrics and a traditional optimizer to find the routing that minimizes
some network metrics (e.g., average delay). Finally, GNNs have been
proposed to learn job scheduling policies in a data-center scenario
without human intervention [49].
</code></pre>
<p><strong>8. Conclusion</strong></p>
<pre><code>In this paper, we presented a DRL architecture based on GNNs that
is able to generalize to unseen network topologies. The use of GNNs
to model the network environment allows the DRL agent to operate
in different networks than those used for training. We believe that the
lack of generalization was the main obstacle preventing the use and
deployment of DRL in production networks. The proposed architecture
represents a first step towards the development of a new generation of
DRL-based products for networking.
In order to show the generalization capabilities of our DRL+GNN
solution, we selected a classic problem in the field of optical networks.
This served as a baseline benchmark to validate the generalization
performance of our architecture. Our results show that the proposed
DRL+GNN agent is able to effectively operate in networks never seen
during training. Previous DRL solutions based on traditional neural
network architectures were not able to generalize to other topologies.
A fundamental challenge that remains to be addressed towards
the deployment of DRL techniques for self-driving networks is their
black-box nature. DRL does not provide guaranteed performance for
all network scenarios and its operation cannot be understood easily
by humans. As a result, DRL-based solutions are inherently complex
to troubleshoot and debug by network operators. In contrast, com-
puter networks have been built around well-understood analytical and
heuristic techniques, and such mechanisms are based on well-known
assumptions that perform reasonably well across different scenarios.
Such issues are not unique to self-driving networks, but rather common
to the application of machine learning to many critical use-cases, such
as self-driving cars.
</code></pre>
